{{Short description|Branch of astronomy dealing with the motions and gravitational effects of celestial objects}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Celestial mechanics
| image = Solar system orrery inner planets.gif
| caption = Motion of the inner planets of the [[Solar System]].
| topics = [[Classical mechanics]], [[Gravitation]], [[Kepler's laws of planetary motion]], [[Newton's laws of motion]]
}}

'''Celestial mechanics''' is the branch of [[astronomy]] that deals with the [[motion]]s of celestial objects. Historically, it applies principles of [[classical mechanics]] and [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]] to determine the orbits of [[planet]]s, [[natural satellite|moon]]s, [[comet]]s, and [[asteroid]]s. Modern celestial mechanics also incorporates [[general relativity]] and numerical methods for high-precision work.

== Historical development ==

=== Kepler's laws ===
{{Main|Kepler's laws of planetary motion}}
[[Johannes Kepler]] formulated three empirical laws of [[planetary motion]] (1609–1619):
# Planets move in [[ellipse|elliptical]] orbits with the Sun at one focus
# A line connecting a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal times
# The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis: <math>T^2 \propto a^3</math>

=== Newtonian synthesis ===
[[Isaac Newton]]'s ''[[Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica]]'' (1687) derived Kepler's laws from universal gravitation and his three [[Newton's laws of motion|laws of motion]], unifying celestial and terrestrial mechanics.

== Fundamentals ==

=== Two-body problem ===
The motion of two bodies under mutual gravitational attraction has an exact analytical solution. The relative motion is equivalent to a single body moving in a central gravitational field:
:<math>\frac{d^2\mathbf{r}}{dt^2} = -\frac{G(m_1 + m_2)}{r^3}\mathbf{r}</math>

The orbit is a [[conic section]]: ellipse, parabola, or hyperbola.

=== Orbital elements ===
An orbit is fully described by six [[orbital elements]]:
* Semi-major axis <math>a</math>
* [[Eccentricity (orbit)|Eccentricity]] <math>e</math>
* Inclination <math>i</math>
* Longitude of the ascending node <math>\Omega</math>
* Argument of periapsis <math>\omega</math>
* True anomaly <math>\nu</math>

=== Three-body and n-body problems ===
For three or more bodies, no general analytical solution exists. The [[three-body problem]] leads to chaotic behaviour and requires numerical integration.

== Perturbation theory ==
Small deviations from idealised two-body motion are treated using [[perturbation theory]]:
* Planetary perturbations — mutual gravitational effects between planets
* Non-spherical gravity — planetary oblateness (<math>J_2</math> effects)
* Relativistic corrections — e.g., Mercury's [[perihelion precession]]

== Modern applications ==
* [[Spacecraft]] trajectory design and navigation
* [[Satellite]] orbit determination
* [[Exoplanet]] detection via [[radial velocity method]]
* [[Asteroid impact prediction]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Classical mechanics]]
* [[Gravitation]]
* [[Kepler's laws of planetary motion]]
* [[Newton's laws of motion]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Celestial mechanics| ]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Astronomy]]
[[Category:Orbits]]
